rule: Cursor-based paging for any collection that can grow
why: B scopes mandatory cursor pagination to resources that grow beyond one page, implicitly permitting offset for small bounded lists.

rule: Cursors are opaque tokens — clients never parse
why: B states cursors are opaque, non-parseable, unguessable tokens clients must not construct.

rule: The sort underlying a cursor is stable and
why: B requires cursors to encode a unique tiebreaker sort key so pagination is stable under duplicates and concurrent writes.

rule: Default page size 50, maximum 200; requests above
why: A mandates clamp-only (never reject) above max; B explicitly permits reject-with-400 as an equally valid alternative to clamping.

rule: The response carries `next_cursor` (null on the last
why: B requires next_cursor be null/absent on the last page and forbids inferring end-of-list from a short-page heuristic.

rule: Filters and sort order are baked into the
why: B requires the cursor to encode/validate filter and sort state, rejecting or restarting pagination on mid-iteration changes.
